nativeRange.habitat: Deep reef and rocky areas at roughly 30–60 m depth; tends to shelter in caves, crevices, and overhangs on reef walls.

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting meaty marine foods in captivity, including mysis shrimp, marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, and quality frozen preparations. Trophic level of 3.6 indicates a carnivorous/piscivorous diet in the wild.

avoidWith: very large predatory fish (lionfish, groupers, eels)

summary: A small, brilliantly colored deep-water cave basslet from the Caribbean, generally considered easy to keep and reef-safe. Tends to be shy and appreciates well-aquascaped caves and rockwork with subdued lighting.
careNotes:
The Caribbean Deepwater Cave Basslet is generally regarded as one of the easier reef fish to keep, though its deep-water origins mean specimens tend to appreciate subdued lighting and well-aquascaped tanks with numerous caves, crevices, and overhangs. In the wild it inhabits reef walls and rocky habitats at 30–60 m depth, so acclimation can take time — newly introduced fish may hide for extended periods before venturing into open water.

Has been reported to be peaceful toward most non-similar fish, but specimens have been known to be territorial toward conspecifics and other similarly shaped basslets. Generally advised to keep only one per tank unless the system is quite large with multiple distinct cave territories.

IMPORTANT — Liopropoma species (soapfishes and cave basslets) are documented to release grammistin, a stress-induced skin toxin, which can be acutely toxic to other tank inhabitants at sufficient concentrations. While individual risk depends on tank volume and water flow, this trait warrants extra care during handling, acclimation, and if a specimen dies unexpectedly. A tight-fitting lid is also advisable, as the species has some jumping potential.

Cross-source notes: Temperature — LiveAquaria gives 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase gives 20.9–27.5°C. The averaged range of 21–27°C is used. Adult size: LiveAquaria states 3.5 in (8.9 cm); FishBase gives 9.0 cm max TL — sources are in agreement. Tank size: LiveAquaria recommends 15 gal (≈57 L); the nearest nice-value from the approved list is 75 L, applied here. Family: LiveAquaria lists 'Grammidae' but FishBase confirms the correct family is Liopropomatidae (Painted basslets) — Liopropomatidae is used.

TOXICITY RECONCILIATION (2026-06-03): fish.toxicToTank corrected from true to false. Grammistin is a defensive skin toxin restricted to the soapfish tribes Grammistini and Diploprionini (genera Grammistes, Rypticus, Diploprion) — Liopropoma species (subfamily Liopropomatinae) do not produce grammistin and are not toxic to tankmates. The earlier true value was inferred from the colloquial "basslet" / "soapfish" family association rather than from species-specific toxinology. Reconciled with sibling Liopropoma entries (Deepwater Candy Basslet, Swales’ Swissguard Basslet) which correctly set this field to false.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Liopropomatidae, order Perciformes/Serranoidei), max size (9.0 cm TL), temperature range (20.9–27.5°C), native range (Western Atlantic: Bermuda, southern Florida, Bahamas, northern South America), depth (30–60 m), and behavior (solitary, rocky reef habitat). FishBase is authoritative on the family classification — LiveAquaria's listing of 'Grammidae' appears to be a retailer error.
